title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Siemens Industrial Gas Turbines Fuel flexibility and alternative fuels for gas turbines Brian M Igoe, Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd. October 2008 NRC-CNRC & IAGT Fall Forum 2008 Ottawa 20-21st October 2008 Page 2 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Opening Question What do you, the audience, think are alternative fuels; Is there room for alternatives to NG or distillate, noting the quantity of fuel necessary to sustain the current gas turbine fleet … ... or is the role of alternative fuels just going to provide a “niche” market What about the “other players for alternative fuels”; the transport sector for example requires large quantities of non-fossil fuels to supplement the fossil fuel demands And … …let’s not forget the other factors such as fuel quality required by GT OEM’s Page 3 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: Fuel Flexible • Steam / Water Injection Page 14 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gasification Gasification and Pyrolysis are not new technology, but biomass and waste are new applications. Many different techniques employed depending on feedstock, i.e.  Atmospheric Circulating Fluidised Bed  Pressurised Circulating Fluidised Bed  Indirectly Heated  Fixed Bed (Updraft and Downdraft)  Bubbling bed  Pyrolysis  Entrained Flow Challenges & opportunities associated with alternative fuels Page 15 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gasification Different processes produce gases of differing calorific values  Medium CV 15 - 37 MJ/Nm3 (pyrolysis and indirectly heated gasifiers)  Low CV 7 - 15 MJ/Nm3 (oxygen blown and indirectly heated gasifiers)  Very low CV 3 - 7 MJ/Nm3 (air blown gasification) This affects turbine combustor configuration, turbine performance and overall plant efficiencies (and unit availability) Choice of gasification system depends on feedstock and application Choice between atmospheric and pressurized systems can affect plant NOx emissions Page 16 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gasification - Medium CV Atmospheric Processes Typically :  Indirectly Heated Gasifier (e.g. FERCO)  Pyrolysis Kiln (e.g. Techtrade, JND, GEM) Advantages  Simplicity of concept  Removal of ammonia etc. pre-turbine, so low NOx and ‘clean’ gas to GT  Reduced gas compression power compared to low CV processes

title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: Gasification - Medium CV Atmospheric Processes Typically :  Indirectly Heated Gasifier (e.g. FERCO)  Pyrolysis Kiln (e.g. Techtrade, JND, GEM) Advantages  Simplicity of concept  Removal of ammonia etc. pre-turbine, so low NOx and ‘clean’ gas to GT  Reduced gas compression power compared to low CV processes Disadvantages  High tars from pyrolysis  Lower tars from indirect gasification, but still requirement for cracking  Complex gas cleaning systems, waste water disposal  Parasitic load of gas compressor, slightly reduced GT output & efficiency Page 17 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Progress  Combustion difficulties  Market survey identified little appetite for such capability  However, MCV fuels from gasification in absence of air/O2 appeared to be gaining ground in terms of technology  BIGCC programme modified to accommodate MCV instead of LCV fuels  Status  Combustion programme confirmed issues and concerns with DLE and flashback  Non DLE (diffusion flame) demonstrated superior capability  Compressor improvements addressing potential surge limit issues completed  Market study completed  All potential gasification/pyrolysis process covered  Potential for IGCC or BIGCC plant still very limited  Lower technology solutions still preferred. Technical Challenge Page 20 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. World's first Biomass Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (BIGCC) scheme  ca. 4000 hours operational experience on BIGCC  4MW SGT-100 (Typhoon) gas turbine, with 2MW steam turbine, producing 6MWe and 9MWth for district heating scheme  Feedstocks tested include wood, forestry wastes, wood/bark mixtures, straw, Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)/Wood chip  Measured electrical efficiency 32%. Scheme built today with current technology would achieve c.40% Varnamo Page 21 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens

All rights reserved. Fuel Quality  Fuel quality must be recognised when sourcing fuels  Equally applicable to pipeline fuels as well as to these alternative fuels  The following notes provide some of the issues and concerns associated with various aspects of fuel quality. This subject is a major one in its own right and should be treated as such when reviewing alternative fuels  Fuel is not the only source of contamination, and all fluids entering the gas turbine must be equally assessed. Aspects of air, fuel, lube oil, water … must be equally be considered. Page 32 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gas Fuels  The quality and composition of gaseous fuels

title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: entering the gas turbine must be equally assessed. Aspects of air, fuel, lube oil, water … must be equally be considered. Page 32 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gas Fuels  The quality and composition of gaseous fuels can impact gas turbine operation, including combustor and hot gas path life.  Changes in quality of gas fuels can lead to operational difficulties such as stability or combustion dynamics under both steady state and transient conditions.  For example a moving from pipeline quality gas fuels to these ‘alternative’ may require additional processing and pre-treatment to make them suitable for use in a gas turbine application. Also, combustion control parameter settings will have to change along with changes to the combustion hardware.  Recognition of fuel species is necessary in terms of additional monitoring equipment, such as that associated with Hydrogen fuels. Page 33 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gas Fuels Contamination Issues Operational concern Effect Solids in gas fuel ¾ Scale, rust, sand, dirt, weld splatter, grit blast ¾ From old poorly maintained pipe system ¾ From new or modified fuel system ¾ Wear of fuel system component ¾ Valve failure to seat increased leakage ¾ Corrosion and wear of fuel injector ¾ Erosion of fuel/combustion components ¾ Build up of debris in gas passageways - impaired operation Heavy Hydrocarbons as liquids ¾ Incorrect process control

title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: From new or modified fuel system ¾ Wear of fuel system component ¾ Valve failure to seat increased leakage ¾ Corrosion and wear of fuel injector ¾ Erosion of fuel/combustion components ¾ Build up of debris in gas passageways - impaired operation Heavy Hydrocarbons as liquids ¾ Incorrect process control ¾ Not present in pipeline quality gases ¾ Incorrect temperature for fuel dew point ¾ Over fuelling (uncontrolled) ¾ Can drop out in fuel system, resulting in poor fuel control ¾ Carried in combustion resulting in uncontrolled combustion - explosions, flashback ¾ Abnormal distribution and localised hot gas path component damage ¾ Coking of fuel burner passages and mal distribution ¾ Abnormal temperature spread, as seen in exhaust / interduct thermocouples ¾ Adverse impact on performance and emission targets Water in gas fuel ¾ Affinity of other contaminant - eg sodium, calcium etc ¾ Acid formation ¾ Formation of Hydrates ¾ At low temperature can freeze resulting in pipe blockage and reduced gas flow ¾ ingestion of liquids into combustion with consequential damage ¾ Ice & Hydrates can cause valve failure ¾ Corrosion of pipe system and valve ¾ Corrosion of hot turbine components ¾ Poor combustion operation, including loss of flame ¾ Unstable operation Page 34 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gas Fuels Contamination Issues Operational concern Effect Gas Fuels containing Hydrogen Sulphide H2S ¾ Poisonous even in small quantities ¾ Flammable ¾ Acidic when water present

title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: of flame ¾ Unstable operation Page 34 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Gas Fuels Contamination Issues Operational concern Effect Gas Fuels containing Hydrogen Sulphide H2S ¾ Poisonous even in small quantities ¾ Flammable ¾ Acidic when water present ¾ Corrosive ¾ Harmful to personnel ¾ Can result in hot gas component erosion ¾ Sulphidation attack on some materials ¾ Increased component attack in the presence of other contaminants, such as Sodium Gas Fuels containing Carbon Dioxide, CO2 ¾ Acidic when water present ¾ Lowers effective heating value of fuel ¾ Reduced output for same volume input (lower heating value) ¾ Increased supply pressure ¾ Combustor passage size increase Gas fuels containing Hydrogen, H2 ¾ Increased flammability ¾ Explosive ¾ Leakage of pipe work - consider regulations - eg Group 2C approval ¾ Explosive ¾ System design - flange joints and seals - embrittlement Gas fuels containing Carbon Monoxide, CO ¾ Poisonous ¾ Exacerbates flame velocity, especially if H2 present ¾ Flash back ¾ Harmful to personnel ¾ Flashback results in damage to combustion components Page 35 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. All rights reserved. Summary Siemens (and as part of Alstom beforehand) have developed alternative fuels capabilities. Access made to UK government funding, and with several external

title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: Forest Waste A Significant Player in Fuel Diversification Page 39 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Summary Siemens (and as part of Alstom beforehand) have developed alternative fuels capabilities. Access made to UK government funding, and with several external bodies/agencies in collaborative projects Many of the technical challenges have been met and overcome, however, there are still many more to be met. (Some associated with clean-up to achieve GT specifications for fuel quality have yet to be met) One fundamental problem still exists and that is market acceptance for a GT based BIGCC solution. Market study completed serves to demonstrate this. This leaves the other types of opportunities to be considered, such as using waste gases – high inert content and process gases such as COG and H2 . Many such projects have shown to offer both environmental as well as economic benefits. New opportunities / projects using high inert containing methane fuels such as Landfill gas or gas derived from anaerobic digestion seem to be a growing trend. Page 40 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved. Questions to consider - SynGas Does the audience think the generation of syngas through gasification or pyrolysis processes is sufficiently mature and free of operational problems to make a BIGCC solution a viable or tenable application. Should such projects be aimed at the gas turbine market, or should such processes as

title: 08-1-3 Brian Igoe - Fuel Flexibility and Allternative Fuels for Gas Turbines
text: to consider - SynGas Does the audience think the generation of syngas through gasification or pyrolysis processes is sufficiently mature and free of operational problems to make a BIGCC solution a viable or tenable application. Should such projects be aimed at the gas turbine market, or should such processes as “fast pyrolisis” be used to provide liquid fuels suitable for transportation use Should the uses of gases such as landfill gas be classed as renewable fuels, or should more effort be made in re-cycling thus minimising the amount of rubbish sent to landfill. As a supplementary question if recycling is maximised and there is still waste left should this go to landfill or should it be offered as a feedstock for incineration (either simple burning via Fluidised Bed process or via pyrolysis) Page 41 Sept 08 IAGT Fall Forum Energy Sector Copyright © Siemens Industrial Turbomachinery Ltd 2008. All rights reserved.
